blocks.text: If $z$ is a function of $x$ and $y$, i.e., $z = f(x, y)$, the total differential $dz$ is obtained from the partial differentials $dx$ and $dy$ by the use of the following relation:

blocks.latex: dz = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} \cdot dx + \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} \cdot dy
blocks.description: This equation represents the total differential of a function $z$ in terms of its partial derivatives with respect to $x$ and $y$.

blocks.text: The reason for this is more clearly seen if we work from the fundamental idea of change, and introduce the actually measurable quantities like $x$ and $y$.

blocks.caption: Figure 21
blocks.description: This figure illustrates the change in $z$ as a function of changes in $x$ and $y$ using the total differential, where point $P$ represents the initial position and point $Q$ represents the new position after a change in values.

blocks.text:
Thus:

$\text{change in } z = \text{change in } z / \text{change in } x \cdot \text{change in } x + \text{change in } z / \text{change in } y \cdot \text{change in } y.$

The change in $z$ due to a change in $x$ and $y$ can be measured by the product of the change in $x$ multiplied by the rate at which $z$ is changing with respect to $x$; and this fact can be better illustrated by referring to a diagram (Fig. 21).

Let $P$ be a point $(x, y)$ on a surface, and let $P$ move to a new position $Q$ near to $P$. The change of position is denoted by $dx$ and $dy$ on the surface (by keeping $z$ constant).
